_doc: Narrative tour presets for the wiki explorer. One canonical story with three durations (short/deep/expansive). Steps are grouped into floors; floors carry hero moments; counter-narratives live at the top level and are referenced from steps.

deep.label: Deep
deep.description: Evidence-rich pass with key diagnostics and constraints.

expansive.label: Expansive
expansive.description: Full chapter mode with methods, assumptions, and alternatives.

counter_narratives.claim: Nepal has about 83 GW of hydropower potential.
counter_narratives.reality: That number is theoretical. Technical potential is much smaller, and economically developable capacity — once grid, storage, finance, and social constraints are applied — is a fraction of it.

counter_narratives.claim: Nepal's energy problem is that it doesn't generate enough electricity.
counter_narratives.reality: Generation has grown sharply. The binding constraints have shifted to storage (reshaping the year) and transmission (moving what is already generated).

counter_narratives.claim: Run-of-river hydropower is enough to meet Nepal's needs.
counter_narratives.reality: Run-of-river mirrors the hydrograph; it cannot reschedule the monsoon. Without storage, firm dry-season supply and dispatch flexibility stay structurally constrained.

counter_narratives.claim: Exporting power to India is Nepal's biggest energy opportunity.
counter_narratives.reality: Timing mismatch tends to push cheap monsoon surplus out and pull expensive dry-season power back in. Domestic industrial use may capture more value per MWh than seasonal trade does.

counter_narratives.claim: Sitting upstream of 500 million people gives Nepal major geopolitical leverage.
counter_narratives.reality: Upstream geography is real but often overstated. Realized leverage depends on infrastructure, institutions, and treaties — not just on being upstream.

master-thesis.title: Nepal Energy Systems Tour
master-thesis.thesis: Nepal is not resource-poor; it is timing-constrained and system-constrained.

steps.title: The Core Question

mode_copy.short: Start from the national puzzle: huge hydro geography, unstable real delivery.
mode_copy.deep: Frame the system question: where does potential disappear between river and socket?
mode_copy.expansive: Establish scope, definitions, and what counts as evidence in this tour.

metrics: Hydropower-rich geography with persistent reliability and timing constraints
System bottlenecks span storage, transmission, and value capture

steps.transition_to_next: To answer this system question, we start with hydrology because timing of water sets timing of power.
questions: Is Nepal's bottleneck generation, storage, grid, market design, or strategy?
What does 'real energy potential' mean in operational terms?

steps.title: Hydrology Reality
steps.hero_moment: Nepal holds a year's worth of rivers, but delivers most of it in three monsoon months.

mode_copy.short: Water is abundant annually but heavily concentrated in monsoon months.
mode_copy.deep: Show basin-by-basin variability and where dry-season firmness breaks down.
mode_copy.expansive: Add measurement caveats, basin exceptions, and climate-sensitivity notes.

metrics: Strong monsoon concentration and weaker dry-season flow in key basins
Hydrological variability differs by basin and elevation profile

steps.transition_to_next: Once the flow profile is clear, the next question is how Nepal's project fleet converts that water into electricity.
questions: Which basins stay resilient in dry season?
How does seasonal concentration shape power design choices?

steps.title: Hydropower Footprint
steps.hero_moment: Nepal's hydropower fleet is a mirror of its rivers: it rises and falls with the monsoon, by design.

mode_copy.short: Most projects are run-of-river, so generation follows flow timing.
mode_copy.deep: Map operating vs construction clusters and explain placement tradeoffs.
mode_copy.expansive: Compare project archetypes, basin concentration, and underused geographies.

metrics: Fleet distribution is uneven across basins and corridor access

steps.transition_to_next: If generation follows river timing, storage becomes the key mechanism for reshaping seasonal energy into firm supply.
questions: Where is the fleet concentrated relative to river quality?
What share of potential is theoretical vs developable?

steps.title: Storage Gap
steps.hero_moment: The missing machine is not more turbines. It is a year-shaped vessel to hold the monsoon.

mode_copy.short: Storage is the missing machine that turns seasonal water into firm power.
mode_copy.deep: Connect monsoon spill, dry-season imports, and reservoir alternatives.
mode_copy.expansive: Include social/environmental tradeoffs and candidate-site prioritization logic.

metrics: Low effective regulation capacity compared with seasonal hydrology
Storage shortlist points to high-leverage dry-season uplift opportunities

steps.transition_to_next: Even with better storage planning, value is still lost if the grid cannot evacuate and route power when available.
questions: How much instability is a storage problem?
Which storage sites create the most system value?

steps.title: Grid Constraint Layer
steps.hero_moment: Megawatts are born in the mountains and die at the substations that cannot move them.

mode_copy.short: Even when electricity is generated, weak corridors and interconnectors bottleneck delivery.
mode_copy.deep: Show where evacuation constraints strand generation and force curtailment.
mode_copy.expansive: Layer corridor timelines, phasing risk, and grid investment sequencing.

metrics: Transmission corridors and interconnections lag generation growth
Evacuation bottlenecks drive curtailment and reliability gaps

steps.transition_to_next: Grid constraints are not only technical; they shape trade timing, contract value, and where electricity creates economic return.
questions: What share of underperformance is network-limited?
Which corridors are highest leverage for reliability and exports?

steps.title: Economics and Timing

mode_copy.short: Timing mismatch can force low-value exports and high-value imports in opposite seasons.
mode_copy.deep: Tie dispatch limits to contract structure, curtailment, and value leakage.
mode_copy.expansive: Add demand-sector pathways for domestic value capture and industrial load growth.

metrics: Seasonal mismatch can compress export value and inflate import dependence
Infrastructure and market design jointly determine value capture

steps.transition_to_next: Economic timing links directly to regional power politics, where geography and interconnection determine bargaining space.
questions: Where is value lost: contracts, timing, or infrastructure?
Domestic use vs export: where does each MWh create more value?

steps.title: Geopolitical Layer
steps.hero_moment: Nepal sits upstream of half a billion people and often acts downstream of its own geography.

mode_copy.short: Nepal's upstream rivers matter downstream through timing, dry-season flow, flood risk, and storage choices.
mode_copy.deep: Differentiate real leverage from overstated narratives using basin routing, contribution, and downstream dependence.
mode_copy.expansive: Add future storage-buildout scenarios under climate stress and regional water politics.

metrics: Nepal-linked rivers are widely cited as a large share of Ganges dry-season flow
Leverage depends on storage, institutions, treaties, and credible downstream cooperation

steps.transition_to_next: The final step ranks bottlenecks and turns this evidence chain into a concrete strategic sequence.
questions: How much leverage is geographic vs institutional?
How does climate volatility alter bargaining power?

steps.title: Synthesis and Path
steps.hero_moment: Nepal is not short on water. It is short on timing, on wires, and on a design.

mode_copy.short: Conclusion: the first unlock is system design, not more slogans about raw potential.
mode_copy.deep: Rank bottlenecks and define the minimum viable 20-year sequence.
mode_copy.expansive: Present competing strategic pathways and stress-test assumptions.

metrics: Primary bottlenecks are timing and system architecture, not raw river endowment
Sequenced investments in storage + transmission + market strategy compound value

questions: If only one bottleneck is solved first, which unlocks the most progress?
What sequence best converts geography into dependable national value?
